title: Reverse Mortgage Pros and Cons: Honest Assessment
metaTitle: Reverse Mortgage Pros & Cons Canada — Honest Guide
metaDescription: Honest assessment of Canadian reverse mortgages — CHIP and Equitable Bank Flex. Real pros, real cons, and when they actually make sense for retirees.
excerpt: Reverse mortgages get demonized online and oversold by salespeople. Here's the honest middle — when they make sense, and when they don't.

body.heading: What reverse mortgages actually do
paragraphs: A reverse mortgage lets Canadians 55+ convert up to 55% of their home equity into tax-free cash, without monthly payments. Interest accrues on the advanced amount and is repaid in full when the home is sold or transferred — typically as part of estate settlement. You retain ownership; the lender holds a charge against the property.

Two products dominate the Canadian market: CHIP (HomeEquity Bank, since 1986) and Equitable Bank's Flex Reverse Mortgage (since 2021). Both have no-negative-equity guarantees, meaning your estate never owes more than the home is worth at sale.

body.heading: Pros — the genuine ones
bullets: Tax-free cash without selling the home or disrupting investment portfolios
No monthly payments — useful for fixed-income retirees with limited cash flow
No income or credit qualifying — the home and your age do the qualifying
Stay in your home as long as you want; the loan is repaid on sale or estate settlement
Use the cash for anything: travel, helping children, healthcare, retirement income

body.heading: Cons — the real ones
bullets: Higher interest rates than conventional mortgages (typically 1.5–3% above standard residential)
Compounding interest — what you advance today doubles in roughly 10–14 years at typical rates
Reduces what's left for your estate; if leaving inheritance is a priority, plan around this
Setup costs (legal fees, appraisal, registration) of $1,500–$3,500
Once registered, refinancing or breaking has its own costs

body.heading: When reverse mortgages genuinely make sense
paragraphs: You're 70+, want to stay in your home for the long term, have meaningful equity, and need income or a lump sum that conventional financing can't easily provide. You don't have estate-leaving as a primary financial goal. You've considered downsizing and rejected it for non-financial reasons (community, accessibility, attachment to the home).

Reverse mortgages don't make sense for short timelines (selling within 2–3 years), borrowers who'd qualify for a conventional refinance or HELOC at much lower rates, or families where estate value is paramount.

callout.title: Talk to family first
callout.text: A reverse mortgage affects estate planning. Have the conversation with adult children and your accountant before signing — not after.

faqs.question: Will the bank take my home?
faqs.answer: No. You remain the registered owner. The lender holds a charge against the property — same as any mortgage. The loan is repaid when you sell, move out permanently, or as part of estate settlement.

faqs.question: What if my home value drops?
faqs.answer: Both major Canadian reverse mortgages have no-negative-equity guarantees: your estate never owes more than the home is worth at sale, even if the loan balance has exceeded the home's value.

faqs.question: Can I pay it off early?
faqs.answer: Yes, but with prepayment penalties similar to a regular mortgage. Most clients hold to sale or estate settlement; early payoff is rare.
